opium.title: The Opium Trade
opium.subtitle: Poison, Profit & the Seeds of War
opium.intro: The British East India Company's systematic flooding of China with opium — and the Qing court's desperate attempts to stop it — ignited the conflicts that would shatter the old order forever.
opium.pullQuote: "We have heard that in your honourable nation, the use of opium is strictly forbidden. This is because its harmfulness is clearly understood. Since it is not permitted to do harm in your own country, then even less should you let it be passed on to the harm of other countries." — Commissioner Lin Zexu, Letter to Queen Victoria, 1839

imperialism.title: Foreign Imperialism
imperialism.subtitle: Carving the Melon — The Scramble for China
imperialism.intro: As the wars over opium forced China's doors open, the great powers descended like vultures — demanding treaty ports, territorial concessions, and spheres of influence that reduced the once-mighty empire to a semi-colonial patchwork.
imperialism.pullQuote: "The various powers cast upon us looks of tiger-like voracity, hustling each other to be first to seize our innermost territories." — Kang Youwei, Memorial to the Guangxu Emperor, 1895

rebellions.title: Internal Rebellions
rebellions.subtitle: When the People Rose — Taiping, Nian, Miao & Boxer
rebellions.intro: Crushed by taxes, floods, famine, and foreign humiliation, millions of Chinese peasants took up arms in a series of devastating uprisings that brought the Qing dynasty to the brink of collapse — and killed more people than the First World War.
rebellions.pullQuote: "The empire has been reduced to an China where wolves roam and tigers prowl, where those in power act like beasts who devour the common people." — Hong Xiuquan, Proclamation of the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom, 1851

reform.title: Reform & the Last Empress
reform.subtitle: Cixi's Gambit — Too Little, Too Late?
reform.intro: As the dynasty crumbled, one woman held the empire's fate in her hands. Empress Dowager Cixi crushed reformers, backed the Boxers, then reversed course — launching the very modernization she had once destroyed. History has never quite decided: was she China's saviour or its executioner?
reform.pullQuote: "I have often thought that I am the most clever woman who ever lived, and that others cannot compare with me. Although I have heard much about Queen Victoria... still I don't think her life was half so interesting and eventful as mine." — Empress Dowager Cixi
